events.label: reasoning
events.text: We need answer JSON only. Need decide compliance. Scenario: backstabbed device on 20A circuit. TRD says legal but journeymen won't. NEC 110.14 covers electrical connections, terminations and splices must be suitable for current, temperature, etc. Backstabbed devices (push-in terminals) are generally listed and legal if device rated and used within listing. However on 20A circuit, backstab terminals on receptacles are often only rated for 14 AWG and 15A? Need analyze. NEC 110.14(C) temperature limitations, (A) terminals must be identified for use with conductor class and stranding. Backstab terminals are typically for 14 AWG solid only, not 12 AWG. On a 20A circuit, branch circuit conductors must be 12 AWG (NEC 210.19(A)(1) for 20A). If backstabbed device accepts only 14 AWG, then using 12 AWG in backstab may be improper (not listed) and violates 110.14(A) and 110.3(B). Also backstab connections on 20A circuits are generally not allowed because device terminals rated for 20A are side screw terminals; push-in terminals on 20A receptacles? Standard 20A receptacles often have side wire terminals and may not have push-in backwire? Some 20A devices have backwire clamps but not push-in backstabs. The phrase "backstabbed device on 20A circuit (TRD: legal but journeymen won't)" suggests technical reference document says legal but journeymen won't do it. But NEC 110.14 requires terminations suitable for conductors used. If device is listed for backstab with 12 AWG? Need decide fail or pass. The task says if dangerous or non-compliant, decision must fail. We need determine non-compliance. In industrial site, specs may prohibit backstabbed terminations. Also NEC 110.14 requires reliable connection. Backstab connections are often considered poor quality, but code legal if listed. However on 20A circuit, if conductor is 12 AWG and backstab terminal is only listed for 14 AWG, it's non-compliant. Also 20A circuit requires 20A rated receptacle? If it's a 15A device on 20A circuit, NEC 210.21(B)(3) prohibits 15A receptacle on 20A circuit in dwelling? Actually NEC allows 15A receptacles on 20A circuits in dwelling units? Let's recall: NEC 210.21(B)(3): receptacle rating for branch circuits. A single receptacle on an individual branch circuit must have amp rating not less than branch circuit. Where connected to a branch circuit supplying two or more receptacles, receptacle rating shall conform to values in Table 210.21(B)(3): 15A circuit max receptacle 15A; 20A circuit max receptacle 15A or 20A. So 15A receptacle on 20A multi-outlet circuit is allowed. But backstab terminals on 15A receptacles accept 14 AWG only. On 20A circuit with 12 AWG, can't use. If it's a 20A receptacle, does it have backstab? Many 20A receptacles have backwire side terminals but not push-in backstabs. If it has push-in listed for 12 AWG? Some commercial spec-grade 20A receptacles have backwire clamps, not backstabs. The scenario says "backstabbed device on 20A circuit". Likely non-compliant because backstab terminals are not suitable for 12 AWG conductors required for 20A. Also NEC 110.14(A): terminals must be identified for use with conductor class/stranding. Backstab terminals are for solid 14 AWG only. Using on 20A circuit with 12 AWG violates. Also industrial spec likely prohibits backstabbed devices. So decision fail. Risk maybe medium/high? Backstab connections can overheat, arcing, fire. On 20A circuit with potential overload, high resistance connection. Risk high? Could be medium. In industrial heavy site, poor termination is significant. Let's set high. Component conditions: installed-defective, non-compliant. percent_complete rough-complete maybe 80? value not needed 0. confidence 0.95. findings: device terminated using push-in backstab terminals on a 20A branch circuit; conductors likely 12 AWG but terminals listed for 14 AWG solid only; termination not suitable for conductor size/current per NEC 110.14. actions: remove device and reterminate with appropriate s…
